summary: Gruber dismantles a Time magazine piece by Sam Gustin that portrays Google as ascendant and Apple as declining. He methodically debunks the article's key claims: that the Chromebook Pixel threatens Apple's laptops (it received mediocre reviews compared to MacBooks' universal 'Excellent' ratings), and that Google Glass's pre-release buzz signals Apple losing its edge. Gruber argues the piece forces unrelated facts into a preconceived 'Apple is doomed without Steve Jobs' narrative, ignoring that Apple's actual shipping products are critically acclaimed and selling in record numbers. He draws a sharp distinction between Google's strategy of revealing unfinished products and Apple's practice of shipping polished ones.
key_points: The Chromebook Pixel received mediocre reviews ('OK' from CNet) while every recent MacBook earned 'Excellent' ratings, undermining the claim it threatens Apple
Google Glass exists only as a pre-announced demo and $1500 developer preview from a company with a track record of failed hardware (Nexus Q, Google TV)
Apple invented new product categories roughly once every three to six years (iPod 2001, iPhone 2007, iPad 2010), not 'frequently' as critics imply when demanding constant reinvention
Glass generates nerd buzz while Apple products generate mainstream public excitement — these are fundamentally different kinds of buzz
The 'Apple is doomed' narrative forces any available facts — stock price, competitor announcements, iterative improvements — into a predetermined conclusion
money_quotes: The iPod in 2001, iPhone in 2007, iPad (which many critics pooh-poohed as 'just a big iPhone') in 2010. So, so frequently — it's a wonder how we ever kept up with Apple's new category inventions.
In short, the Chromebook Pixel is interesting, but it's not a well-regarded laptop. But it's bad news for Apple, that's for sure.

Glass has nerds excited; Apple products get the general public excited, and often annoy nerds by being iterative improvements that press the 'familiar but better' button instead of the 'new and different' button.

On the one hand, we have actual shipping products from Apple that are critically acclaimed and selling in record numbers (and for record profits); on the other, a $1300 laptop that only runs a web browser and a HUD headset that will supposedly ship at the end of the year from Google.

So a product from Google that currently exists only as a pre-announced demo and somewhat-imminent $1500 developer preview — from the company that brought us the Nexus Q and Google TV — shows that Apple has lost its edge? OK, got it.
Google Glass absolutely is generating buzz, but it's not 'the sort of buzz usually reserved for Apple products'.
key_insight: The 'Apple is doomed without Steve Jobs' narrative is so entrenched that journalists will force any evidence — even mediocrely reviewed competitors and unshipped demos — into supporting it, while ignoring Apple's record-breaking actual results.
